title: Fifteen Tasks But a Game Ain't One!
tagline: Escape the loop of your boring job—and chase your dreams.
description: Created for the GMTK 2025 Game Jam, *Fifteen Tasks But a Game Ain't One!* is a workplace comedy about an employee trapped in an endless routine. Complete a repeating sequence of office tasks while secretly applying for a better job on your phone. Every application brings you closer to freedom—but if the boss catches the phone in view, the run ends.
longDescription:
## Concept

The game was created around the GMTK 2025 theme **Loop**. We interpreted it as the repetitive routine of an unfulfilling job: receive a computer, prepare it, package it, send it away, and immediately begin again.

While completing this routine, the player can hold **Spacebar** to bring out a phone and apply for other jobs. This creates the central tension of the game: continue working to appear productive, or risk being caught while trying to escape.

## My Role

As a **Game Programmer** on a team of three, I worked across the gameplay codebase, with a particular focus on the core work loop, its state machine, object interactions, progression rules, boss behaviour, and escalating difficulty.

Working within a short game-jam timeframe meant building systems that were quick to author while remaining flexible enough to support the complete sequence of tasks.

## The Work Loop

Each work cycle is composed of a fixed sequence of physical interactions:

- Receive and position a package
- Cut and open the box
- Remove the computer
- Insert the USB drive
- Boot and prepare the machine
- Remove the USB drive
- Repack the computer
- Close and tape the box
- Move the completed package onto the conveyor belt

Once the package leaves the workstation, the entire sequence begins again.

Behind the scenes, a state machine controls the active step, positions the required objects, accepts only the correct interaction, and advances the loop when the expected action is completed.

## Work or Freedom

The Freedom meter represents the player's chance of escaping the job. Submitting applications from the phone increases Freedom, while time and the continued routine create pressure to act.

Reaching maximum Freedom means finding a new job and escaping the loop. Letting it fall to zero leaves the player trapped. The phone is therefore both the player's progression tool and their greatest risk.

## The Boss

The boss periodically walks past the workstation and may stop to inspect the player. His movement timing, inspection timing, and probability of checking are randomized. If the phone is visible during an inspection, the player is caught immediately.

The pressure increases when the player remains on the same task for too long. The difficulty system gradually raises both the boss's appearance intensity and the probability of an inspection, preventing the player from waiting indefinitely for a perfectly safe moment.
techHighlights: Multi-step objects with their own internal states
Randomized boss movement with adaptive inspection pressure
Risk-and-reward phone application and Freedom system

codeSnippets.title: Core Task State Machine
codeSnippets.description: The complete work routine is represented as a cycle of connected states. Each state defines which objects are active, where they should appear, and which interaction advances the sequence. Completing the final packaging task returns the state machine to its initial state, allowing the workflow to repeat without rebuilding or manually resetting the scene.

diagram.label: The six stages of the repeating work loop

codeSnippets.title: State-Gated Object Interactions
codeSnippets.description: Workplace objects share a reusable interaction layer that distinguishes clicks from drag operations and validates drops against the current task. The same framework supports boxes, computers, USB drives, cutting tools, and tape while allowing each object to implement its own multi-step behaviour. This excerpt has been condensed and renamed for portfolio readability.

codeSnippets.title: Adaptive Boss Pressure
codeSnippets.description: The boss alternates between randomized waiting, walking, and inspection phases. A separate difficulty system monitors whether the player is progressing through the work loop. Remaining in the same state increases the frequency of appearances and the probability of being inspected, turning hesitation into additional pressure. This excerpt combines and renames the relevant jam code so the complete rule can be read in one place.

codeSnippets.title: Phone and Freedom System
codeSnippets.description: Holding Space slides the phone into view. Each successful application increases Freedom, rotates the displayed job listing, and temporarily disables the Apply button. The phone communicates with the boss system through signals; if an inspection begins while it is visible, the attempt to escape is exposed and the run ends.

codeSnippets.title: Multi-Step Computer Preparation
codeSnippets.description: Preparing each computer is a smaller state sequence: unpacking it, inserting the USB drive, opening and booting the machine, waiting for installation, closing it, removing the drive, and placing it back in the box. Visual states, timed transitions, audio feedback, and object signals make the process feel like a physical desk task rather than a simple progress bar.
